title: Anthropic models back online as US AI export experiment ends, digital tax war escalates
subhead: The US reversed export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models after an 18-day standoff that saw China's Zhipu fill the vacuum with its own open-source release — a sequence that crystallized the closed vs. open AI race. Meanwhile, Trump's blanket threat of 100% tariffs on any country imposing a digital services tax puts the EU on a fresh collision course, and the UK's under-16 social media ban becomes the world's most sweeping platform restriction.

sections.heading: Anthropic models restored after 18-day export control reversal
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department lifted export restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on June 30, ending an 18-day episode that began when Commerce ordered Anthropic to shut off access to both models for all foreign nationals on June 12. Because Anthropic had no way to restrict by nationality inside a live product, it pulled both models globally — cutting even US organizations off from Mythos 5 for two weeks.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick confirmed Anthropic would 'no longer need a license for exports or in-country transfers' of the models, acknowledging the company had taken steps to address the risks that triggered the controls. Anthropic said it expanded its government partnership, created a 24/7 internal team to monitor jailbreak threats, and worked with Amazon, Microsoft, and Google to draft a consensus framework for scoring AI jailbreak severity under the Glasswing program.

Fable 5 is now available globally. Mythos 5 has been restored to US organizations and will expand to 'a broader set of domestic and international partners' through Glasswing, which allows vetted cybersecurity researchers defensive access. Anthropic acknowledged a trade-off: the new safety classifier blocks over 99% of the jailbreak method Amazon researchers flagged, but will also block some benign prompts during routine coding and debugging — those requests will be rerouted to Opus 4.8.

Forbes reports that within a day of the June 12 shutdown, China's Zhipu released GLM-5.2 under an MIT open-source license — now rated by independent analysts as the most capable open-weight model. The sequence highlighted the core tension: US gatekeeping risks pushing global users toward Chinese open alternatives, while open release puts powerful capabilities in anyone's hands.
sections.whyItMatters: The 18-day shutdown was the first real test of US AI export controls on a deployed frontier model. The speed with which China filled the gap — and the US reversal that followed — suggests that unilateral model-level restrictions may be self-defeating in a market where open alternatives exist. The Glasswing red-team framework may become the de facto standard for how governments and companies share security testing of frontier AI.

sections.heading: Trump threatens 100% tariff on any country with a digital services tax
sections.body:
President Trump on June 26 threatened a 100% tariff on all goods from any country that imposes a digital services tax on American companies, one day after EU nations met his July 4 deadline to cut tariffs on US goods. The threat explicitly overrides existing trade deals: 'This TARIFF will supersede Trade Deals made with the Country, whether implemented, signed, or not,' Trump wrote on social media, as reported by Reuters, CNBC, and Politico.

The threat came just days after the earlier France-specific escalation at the G7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, where Trump demanded Macron scrap France's digital services tax or face 100% tariffs on French wine and champagne. Macron refused, telling Reuters that France 'would not bow to pressure.' France's digital services tax, which primarily targets US tech giants like Google, Meta, and Amazon, has been a persistent friction point since its 2019 introduction.

Several EU countries — including Austria, Italy, and Spain — already operate digital services taxes. The UK has one as well. Trump's blanket threat effectively puts the EU's entire trade relationship with the US at risk unless those taxes are dropped, creating a binary choice for European governments between protecting tax revenue from US platforms and maintaining tariff-free market access.
sections.whyItMatters: This is not a narrow France-US dispute anymore. It is a direct confrontation between US tech platform interests and the tax sovereignty of multiple nations. If implemented, 100% tariffs would effectively end transatlantic trade in goods — making this the most coercive trade mechanism yet deployed over digital policy. The EU will have to decide whether to fold on digital taxation or accept economic confrontation.

sections.heading: UK bans social media for under-16s — the world's broadest platform restriction
sections.body:
UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a ban on social media for children under 16, covering Facebook, Instagram, X, TikTok, Snapchat, and YouTube. The ban, expected to take effect in spring 2027, also raises the minimum age for chatbots that imitate romantic interactions to 18 and bans livestreaming features and stranger contact for under-16s across all platforms. Messaging services like WhatsApp and Signal are exempt, Reuters and AP report.

The move follows a public consultation that ran from March to May 2026, attracting over 100,000 submissions. The government announced the ban before publishing the full consultation findings. A former Starmer adviser told Wired the decision was rushed to shore up parliamentary support amid a potential leadership challenge, though the issue also has genuine cross-party and public momentum.

YouTube spokesperson Jay Stoll argued that 'blanket bans push kids out of curated, supervised, beneficial experiences and toward anonymous, less safe services.' Snap's public policy lead Frederika Cook said an outright ban on messaging 'doesn't make teens safer — it may simply push them to less safe platforms.' The UK ban follows Australia's similar legislation from November 2025, but is broader in scope — covering more platforms and adding chatbot age restrictions.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most far-reaching social media restriction for minors anywhere in a major democracy. It sets a precedent that treats platform access itself as harmful, rather than targeting specific features or content types. The implementation mechanics — age verification at scale, platform compliance, enforcement — remain undefined, and the risk of driving teenagers toward less moderated services is the core critique from the platforms themselves.

sections.heading: G7 critical minerals alliance and Singapore's AI-driven export surge
sections.body:
G7 leaders at Évian-les-Bains agreed to form a critical minerals alliance to reduce reliance on China, including aligned stockpiling and a new platform with an expanded role for the International Energy Agency. Reuters reports that the plan covers coordination on diversifying supply chains, stockpiling, and tracking minerals throughout global supply chains. Japan's PM Shigeru Ishiba specifically proposed joint G7 reserves for rare earths to counter China's export curbs, according to Japan Times and Mainichi.

The urgency is tangible. Channel News Asia reports that Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose 38.4% year-on-year in May — the sharpest increase in over two decades — driven almost entirely by AI infrastructure demand. Electronics exports surged 94.8%, with integrated circuits up 80.9%, disk media products up 227.8%, and PCs up 140.9%. eToro analyst Zavier Wong traced the surge to a 'single concentrated force': Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon collectively lifting 2026 capex plans to over US$700 billion.

Meanwhile, Brazil's President Lula told the White House that Brazil's rare earth reserves are open to all partners — but refining will stay in Brazil. Lula's adviser Celso Amorim told a Rio conference that Latin America and Europe are 'bystanders in the US-China AI race' and that the Global South must respond through BRICS, casting rare earths and data as matters of sovereignty.
sections.whyItMatters: The minerals alliance is a coordinated attempt to build an alternative supply chain — but it starts from a position of deep dependency, and stockpiling alone doesn't create new mines or refineries. Singapore's export figures show just how concentrated AI infrastructure demand has become: four hyperscalers are now reshaping global trade flows in semiconductors and storage. Brazil's refusal to pick a side adds another variable — the critical minerals map isn't just US vs. China.

sections.heading: F-35 readiness hits new low; Germany pivots to Team Gen 6 after FCAS collapse
sections.body:
A US Government Accountability Office report found that only 25% of F-35s are fully mission capable, down from 44% at the start of the decade and 67% in earlier years. Defense News and Stars and Stripes report that the Pentagon plans to spend an additional $13.7 billion through 2031 to improve readiness, but constrained industry capacity for parts could 'threaten its success,' per the GAO. The F-35 program executive officer warned that the fleet has outgrown its support system.

In Europe, the future fighter landscape has been reshaped. Germany and Spain launched Team Gen 6 — an Airbus-led consortium of eight defense firms — to develop a sixth-generation fighter after the Franco-German-Spanish FCAS program effectively collapsed. The Conversation reports that France, Germany, and Spain spent nearly a decade on FCAS before Germany pulled the plug. The new consortium still needs political backing, significant funding, and likely more partners to be viable.

Separately, a joint investigation by Nordic and Baltic media found that Russia is expanding military infrastructure near its European borders to accommodate over 100,000 additional troops, according to SVT and Norwegian public broadcasting. And in Sweden, a 34-year-old former Swedish military IT consultant went on trial for attempted espionage for Russia — charged with trying to pass classified information to Russian intelligence services.
sections.whyItMatters: The F-35 readiness figure is striking: three out of four aircraft can't perform their full mission set, even as the jet is the backbone of US and allied air power. The FCAS collapse and Team Gen 6 pivot fragment Europe's next-generation fighter effort — with France likely pursuing its own path. Russia's border infrastructure build-up and the Swedish espionage case underscore that the Nordic-Baltic region is the most active zone for both conventional and hybrid threats.

sections.heading: Social media overtakes TV and news sites as primary news source globally
sections.body:
The Reuters Institute Digital News Report 2026 found that social media and video platforms have overtaken both television and news organizations' own websites as the most popular way people get news worldwide. The report marks 2026 as 'a significant turning point,' with TikTok and Instagram gaining particular influence as news sources. For Generation Z, social media has officially overtaken news websites as the primary source, per Dhaka Tribune's coverage of the same report.

AdNews Australia notes that the shift means traditional news business models are under unprecedented pressure, as advertising revenue follows attention to platforms that don't produce original reporting. The finding reinforces a trend that has been accelerating since 2020, but 2026 is the first year the crossover point has been reached at a global scale.
sections.whyItMatters: When the primary information channel for a generation is platforms optimized for engagement rather than accuracy, the implications cut across governance, election integrity, and tech regulation. The UK's under-16 social media ban, the EU's digital services tax standoff, and the Reuters report are all symptoms of the same underlying shift: platforms are now the dominant public square, and every government is scrambling to decide what that means for regulation.

sections.heading: Japan-South Korea cooperate on LNG carriers; HK-SenseTime AI data center
sections.body:
Japan announced plans to revive domestic LNG carrier construction by 2035, pursuing technological cooperation with South Korea — currently the world's dominant LNG shipbuilder. Japan has not built an LNG carrier since 2019. The Japanese government views securing energy transport capabilities as critical to national security, given that nearly all its LNG imports arrive by sea.

Meanwhile, Hong Kong Science and Technology Park (HKSTP) and SenseTime announced a partnership to build a homegrown AI data center, targeting 40,000 petaflops of computing power by 2030. The project will be built in three stages and aims to drive the sector's industrialization in Hong Kong, per MSN reporting. SenseTime, a Chinese AI firm headquartered and listed in Hong Kong, is positioning the city as a node in China's broader AI infrastructure build-out.

Separately, Applied Materials reported record quarterly revenue and its highest gross margin in over two decades, raising its semiconductor equipment growth outlook to above 30% for 2026 and expanding its Singapore Tampines campus. And a Polsia AI CEO warned that China's open-source models — like GLM-5.2 — performing near frontier levels at a fraction of the cost could deflate the US AI valuation bubble, per MSN.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan's LNG carrier pivot is industrial policy responding to energy security anxiety — it signals that even close US allies are hedging on maritime supply chain dependencies. The SenseTime data center project is a concrete example of how China's AI infrastructure is scaling inside the Greater Bay Area, complementing the open-source model strategy. Applied Materials' results confirm the hyperscaler capex boom is still accelerating, even as pricing pressure from Chinese open models begins to squeeze US AI company margins.

bottomLine: The reversal of Anthropic's export controls after just 18 days — during which China filled the vacuum with an open-source release — is the day's most consequential signal: unilateral model-level restrictions are vulnerable to rapid substitution by open alternatives. Trump's blanket digital services tax threat escalates the tech-trade collision from a France-specific dispute to a systemic US-EV confrontation. The UK's under-16 social media ban and the Reuters Institute finding that social media has overtaken traditional news globally are twin markers of the same shift: platforms are now the dominant public square, and every government is trying to figure out what that means for regulation, taxation, and safety.
sources: Ars Technica, Forbes, Reuters, AP, Wired, Channel News Asia, Defense News, Stars and Stripes, The Conversation, MSN, CNBC, Politico, SCMP, Japan Times, Mainichi, Dhaka Tribune, AdNews Australia, Yahoo News, National Interest, Reuters Institute Digital News Report, eToro, Strait Times, Singapore Business Times
